summary: Dan Shapiro documents his quest to buy decent woodworking chisels after realizing his current set is worthless. After using a friend's mediocre-but-functional Irwin/Marples chisels, he's reminded that his own chisels are even worse—especially after his wife used them to chip cement. He sets a $60 budget and evaluates four options ranging from cheap sets with wine money left over to two premium Japanese chisels rated #1 by Fine Woodworking.
key_points: The realization that even mediocre tools dramatically outperform truly bad ones
Evaluating the classic budget tradeoff: more adequate tools vs. fewer excellent ones
Practical research sharing with links to chisel reviews and terminology guides
The domestic negotiation angle of tool purchasing as a married hobbyist
money_quotes: As soon as the blade hit the wood fibers I was brutally reminded that my chisels are absolute, unmitigated, crap.
Frankly, at this point I'd be better off with a butter knife.
They're not total crap, but they're not the chisels you're going to take to your grave by any means.
Get a set of 4 Blue Chip chisels and blow the extra $35 on a good bottle of wine.
Get two badass chisels that will last forever, and deal with only having 1/4 and 3/4 sizes.
key_insight: When buying tools on a budget, the core decision is whether to get more adequate tools or fewer excellent ones—a tradeoff that applies well beyond woodworking.
